unitTitle: KS1 Y1 Technology around us (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Technology in our classroom — what is technology?
lessons.objectives:
I can say what technology means
I can sort things into technology and not technology
I can find technology in the room
I can say how a piece of technology helps us
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: technology. TA: everyone has the starter and activity worksheet; have a few real objects ready (pencil, scissors, a computer). Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — what is technology? On the starter worksheet pupils CHOOSE the meaning of technology (made by people to help us). Then tick yes/no on a few items.
   • Support: yes/no — is a pencil/tree/computer technology?  • Core: name one piece of technology in the room.  • Challenge: multi-select what makes something technology.
2. (5–15) I-do → we-do — say what technology means. Technology is MADE BY PEOPLE and HELPS US. Show the computer image. Likely error: “technology must be electronic” → fix-words “a pencil is technology too.” A guide dog helps us but people did not make it, so it is not technology.
3. (15–30) Is it technology? Pupils SORT items into technology / not technology on the activity worksheet (card-sort). Scaffolding prompts: “Does it help us? Did people make it?” TA: prompt, do not sort it for them.
4. (30–45) How does it help us? Pupils MATCH each piece of technology to the job it helps with (pencil→write, scissors→cut, phone→call, lamp→see). Use the phrase “it helps us to…”. Then a calm technology hunt; they photograph one piece for show-your-work.
5. (45–50) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; each pupil names one piece of technology and why it is technology.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling — nobody’s sheet looks “less done”. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Using technology — the parts of a computer
lessons.objectives:
I can name the main parts of a computer
I can switch on and log in to a computer
I can use a mouse to click and drag
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: computer · screen · base unit · keyboard · mouse · trackpad. TA: a computer ready per pupil/pair; the drag puzzle (ncce.io/drag) bookmarked. Read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — recap: technology is made by people to help us (single choice on the starter worksheet).
2. (5–15) I-do → we-do — name the parts. Show the desktop image: SCREEN (look at), BASE UNIT (the thinking box), KEYBOARD (type), MOUSE (point/click). Pupils LABEL the computer on the activity worksheet (drag screen/base unit/keyboard/mouse). Show the laptop — same parts in one case. Likely error: “a laptop has no base unit” → fix-words “the laptop keeps the parts inside the case.”
3. (15–20) Online-safety recap (first time online this unit): tell a trusted adult if anything online makes you uncomfortable. Short and reassuring.
4. (20–45) You-do — switch on, log in, use the mouse. Break logging in into small steps; log them in if needed. Open the drag puzzle; model click-hold-drag. Pupils click and drag the pieces and screenshot the puzzle.
   • Support: choose which part you LOOK at / TYPE on / POINT with.  • Core: match each part to its job.  • Challenge: where are the desktop parts on a laptop?
5. (45–50) Plenary — fill in the missing computer parts; self-tick the three “I can…”. Log off safely.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Developing mouse skills
lessons.objectives:
I can use a mouse to open a program
I can click and drag to make marks
I can use a mouse to draw a picture
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: computer · mouse · trackpad · double-click. TA: computers ready; the paint program (paintz.app) bookmarked. Read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — name the mouse (single choice). Recap: mice look different (wires / no wires / trackpad) but do the SAME job.
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — the double-click. A double-click is TWO clicks, fast. Practise off-computer: two quick claps or taps. Then model on a real mouse. Likely error: clicks too slow / too far apart → fix-words “two fast clicks, close together.”
3. (20–30) Open the program. Pupils ORDER the steps on the activity worksheet (log in → double-click browser → type address → open paint). Model it. TA: prompt the steps, do not click for them.
4. (30–45) You-do — draw with the mouse. Click and DRAG to make a mark; change colour and thickness. Pupils draw a simple picture and screenshot it.
   • Support: choose how you open a program / make a mark.  • Core: how do you make a line in the paint program?  • Challenge: name a tool you used to change your picture.
5. (45–50) Plenary — show an example; which tool changed it (thicker line, new colour, fill)? Self-tick the three “I can…”.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Using a computer keyboard
lessons.objectives:
I can say what a keyboard is for
I can type my name on a computer
I can save my work to a file
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: computer · keyboard · mouse · typing · save. TA: computers ready; the paint program from last lesson bookmarked. Read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — what is a keyboard for? Typing = writing letters, words and numbers on a screen (single choice). Keyboards look different but do the same job.
2. (10–30) I-do → we-do — type your name. Open the paint program, click the text tool (T), drag a text box, type your name. Note: clicking away finalises the box. TA: prompt, do not type for them.
3. (30–40) Save your work. Pupils ORDER the save steps on the activity worksheet (click save icon → type a sensible name → keep .png → save). The save icon looks the same in many programs.
   • Support: choose which part you type with / which button saves.  • Core: a good, sensible file name.  • Challenge: multi-select why a sensible name helps.
4. (40–45) Why a sensible name? So you can find it again and know what it is without opening it. Pupils screenshot their typed name.
5. (45–50) Plenary — review one saved file; which tools were used? Self-tick the three “I can…”.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Developing keyboard skills — editing text
lessons.objectives:
I can open my work from a file
I can use the arrow keys to move the cursor
I can delete letters
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: keyboard · computer · cursor · backspace. TA: computers ready; the printed keyboard/mouse cards for the recap; pupils’ saved files findable. Read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — mouse or keyboard? Quick statements; pupils move to the matching device. On the starter worksheet they choose mouse/keyboard and the right key.
2. (10–20) Open your saved file. Pupils ORDER the open-a-file steps on the activity worksheet (open paint → click open icon → find file by name → open). Remind them WHY a sensible name helped.
3. (20–40) I-do → we-do — the keys that help you write. Pupils LABEL the keyboard image (space bar, backspace, arrow keys). The cursor is the blinking line. Model: type a sentence, then fix a letter with arrow keys + backspace. Likely error: holding backspace too long → fix-words “one tap, one letter.” Scaffold sheet gives ready-made sentences.
   • Support: which key makes a space / deletes a letter.  • Core: which key moves the cursor to fix a word.  • Challenge: write the steps to delete a letter and retype it.
4. (40–45) You-do — write and fix a short sentence under the picture; screenshot it.
5. (45–50) Plenary — think-pair-share “why give a file a sensible name?” Self-tick the three “I can…”.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Using a computer responsibly
lessons.objectives:
I can say why we have rules
I can give a rule for using technology safely
I can say how a rule helps us
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: computer · technology · rules · safe. TA: large paper/flipchart for the class rules; the poster scaffold template ready. Read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — why do we have rules? Rules help us, keep us safe, happy and learning (single choice). Gather the class’s school rules on the board.
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — rules for computers. Build a list WITH the class: ask an adult if unsure; tell a trusted adult if something upsets you; take a break; be kind in messages. Pupils SORT choices into safe / not a safe choice on the activity worksheet (card-sort).
3. (20–40) How does the rule help? Pupils MATCH each rule to why it helps (break→healthy, kind→happy, save→work safe). Then groups make a poster for one safe rule (scaffold template available) and photograph it for show-your-work.
   • Support: choose a good rule / what to do if worried online.  • Core: match each rule to why it helps.  • Challenge: write a rule and say how it helps.
4. (40–50) Plenary — end of unit. Recap the journey: technology is made by people to help us → the parts of a computer → mouse and keyboard → using a computer safely. Self-tick the three “I can…”.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.
